does: This paper develops a sparse Mixture-of-Experts reward model for learning personalized preference representations from binary preference data in RLHF. The key contribution is introducing sparsity constraints and expert diversity regularization during training to encourage each expert to specialize in coherent preference patterns rather than learning redundant or entangled features. The authors demonstrate across controlled and real-world experiments that their sparse MoE approach produces interpretable routing decisions, learns genuinely specialized experts corresponding to distinct preference dimensions, and achieves improved test-time personalization compared to dense mixture approaches. The method works without requiring additional human annotations beyond existing pairwise preference labels.
connects: This work builds on recent efforts to move beyond single universal reward functions in RLHF by incorporating mixture-of-experts architectures for preference modeling. It extends prior work on learning multiple preference components from binary data but addresses a critical limitation: existing component-based approaches typically learn uninterpretable or overlapping expert representations. The sparse MoE formulation draws inspiration from successful applications of mixture-of-experts in other ML domains where sparsity improved interpretability and specialization. This research also connects to broader work on disentangled representation learning and the long-standing challenge of capturing heterogeneous human preferences in alignment, building on foundations laid by preference learning literature in RLHF.
enables: This approach enables several future directions in interpretable and adaptive alignment. First, it opens pathways for understanding and auditing which preference dimensions LLMs learn, making reward models more transparent for safety analysis. Second, the interpretable expert structure could support more principled personalization strategies where users are matched to specialized experts or preferences are decomposed by human-understandable concepts. Third, the method suggests that sparsity and interpretability can coexist with scaling in reward modeling, potentially improving sample efficiency in preference learning. The framework also hints at applications in multi-stakeholder alignment where different experts could represent distinct groups or values, and enables post-hoc analysis of how models adapt to individual preferences through shifts in routing weights.
